content.text: is an Anthropic API feature that lets you designate a static prefix of your request — system prompt, tool definitions, long documents, few-shot examples — as a cacheable block. The first request computes and stores the transformer key-value (KV) attention states for that prefix on Anthropic's infrastructure. Every subsequent request that sends the same prefix pays only about 10 % of the normal input-token cost for those tokens and receives a faster time-to-first-token (TTFT), because the expensive attention computation is skipped.
You mark a cacheable block by adding

to the last content block of the prefix. The Anthropic SDK forwards this marker to the API, which checks whether a matching KV cache exists. On a miss it computes and stores the cache (with a one-time 25 % surcharge on the prefix). On a hit it loads the cached states instead of recomputing them.

Prompt caching is available on Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Claude 3 Opus, and Claude 3 Haiku. It is specifically a feature of Anthropic's API — not a property of the Claude model weights themselves — and the cache is isolated per API key.

LLM inference cost is dominated by input tokens. A typical agentic system sends a large, stable context on every call: a detailed system prompt explaining the agent's role and constraints (often 1,000–5,000 tokens), a full set of tool definitions (several hundred tokens each), and sometimes retrieved documents or few-shot examples. For an agent that handles 1,000 requests per day against a 4,000-token prefix, that is 4 million prefix tokens billed per day — even though the prefix content never changes.

Prompt caching collapses that to roughly 400,000 token-equivalents per day (90 % reduction), after the first write per five-minute window. At Claude 3.5 Sonnet prices this is the difference between tens and hundreds of dollars per million API requests. At production scale — millions of requests — caching can determine whether a product's unit economics are viable.

. The model does not need to attend over the cached prefix tokens during prefill; instead it loads precomputed KV states and only processes the new tokens. For long prefixes (several thousand tokens), this shortens TTFT noticeably — reducing the gap between calling the API and seeing the first output token.
Up to 90 % cost reduction on the prefix.
Cache hits are charged at ~10 % of the normal input-token price for the cached portion, leaving only the new dynamic tokens (user query, tool results) billed at full rate.
Skipping prefill computation over thousands of cached tokens reduces the latency between sending a request and receiving the start of the response.

You can include lengthy reference documents, extensive tool definitions, or detailed role descriptions without being penalized on every call. Caching makes it economical to give the model more context.
The model produces identical outputs whether the prefix was served from cache or computed fresh. There is no accuracy or quality trade-off.
Each cache hit resets the five-minute TTL. A steady stream of requests keeps the cache alive indefinitely with no manual management.
The cache is managed entirely by Anthropic's API layer. You do not run a cache server, configure eviction policies, or handle invalidation logic.

Prompt caching is not free on first use. The initial request (cache write) is charged 125 % of the normal input-token rate for the prefix — a 25 % surcharge. If you cache a 4,400-token prefix, the first request costs 5,500 token-equivalents instead of 4,400. You break even after a single cache hit; beyond that, every hit saves 90 %. The surcharge matters only for workflows where cache TTL expires before enough requests arrive to amortize it.

The cache expires after five minutes of inactivity. Low-traffic APIs — fewer than one request per five minutes — will mostly experience cache misses and pay the write surcharge repeatedly without recouping savings.

The API keys the cache on the exact content of the prefix block. Even a single character difference (whitespace, punctuation, a changed model version string) causes a cache miss and a new write. Dynamic content must not appear inside the cached block.
A cacheable block must contain at least 1,024 tokens for Claude 3.5 Sonnet and Claude 3 Opus, or 2,048 tokens for Claude 3 Haiku. Shorter prefixes are ineligible and

The cache applies to a prefix, not arbitrary substrings. You cannot cache the middle of a conversation or a suffix. The cacheable block must begin at the start of the request (or after the system prompt, for message-level caching).
is supported — a rolling five-minute TTL. There is no persistent cache option with a longer lifetime as of mid-2025.
Cache entries are not shared between different API keys, even for identical content. Organizations using separate keys per environment pay separate write costs.

During normal LLM inference, every input token must pass through all attention layers of the transformer: the model computes query, key, and value vectors for each token and attends across all previous positions. For a 4,000-token prefix this is the dominant cost — the quadratic attention over the full context. Prompt caching short-circuits this by storing the computed
for the prefix after the first request.

On subsequent requests, instead of recomputing KV pairs for the cached tokens, the API loads the stored tensors directly into the attention cache. The model then processes only the new tokens (user query, tool results) — attending over the loaded KV states as if it had computed them itself. The outputs are numerically identical to a fresh computation.
You signal where the cacheable prefix ends by placing
on the last content block of the prefix. In the Anthropic Python SDK, this appears as a

key on a message content item or system prompt block. The API inspects this marker, hashes the prefix content, and either stores a new cache entry (miss) or retrieves an existing one (hit). The response includes
fields so you can observe caching behavior.

. If requests arrive at any rate above once per five minutes, the cache stays alive indefinitely. If traffic stops for more than five minutes, the cache expires and the next request pays the write surcharge again.

attrs.alt: Prompt caching flow: Request 1 (cache miss) sends full prefix + query, API computes KV cache and stores it. Requests 2-N (cache hit) send the same prefix marker + new query; API loads cached KV tensors, charging ~10% of prefix tokens.

content.text: The diagram contrasts two execution paths. On a

(first request), the full prefix — system prompt, tool definitions, few-shot examples — travels to the API, which computes its KV tensors and stores them in the KV Cache Store. The prefix write surcharge applies. On a

(all subsequent requests), the request body still includes the prefix (the API needs it to verify the hash), but the API loads KV tensors from cache rather than recomputing them. Only the new dynamic tokens are fully processed at the normal rate. The cache store is keyed by the exact prefix hash and scoped to the API key.

Prompt caching pays off whenever you send the same large static prefix on many requests within a five-minute window. The break-even point is a single cache hit: one hit recoups the 25 % write surcharge and all further hits are net savings.

An agent with a detailed system prompt (role definition, constraints, output format rules) that is identical across all user sessions is a natural caching candidate. A 2,000-token system prompt cached over 100 requests saves ~1.8 million token-equivalents.
Tool definitions for ten or twenty tools can easily reach 2,000–4,000 tokens. These definitions change only when the tool API changes, making them ideal for caching.
Sending a long document (contract, codebase, research paper) followed by multiple questions: cache the document block and only pay full price for each new question.
If you maintain a fixed set of examples to guide the model's output format, cache the example bank and pay only for the dynamic user input.
An orchestrator that fans out to many worker calls, all sharing the same task specification and context, benefits significantly from caching that shared prefix.

Prompt caching is less effective when the prefix changes on every request (personalized per-user context), when traffic is too sparse (fewer than one request per TTL window), or when the prefix is below the minimum token threshold (under 1,024 tokens for Sonnet/Opus).
Install the Anthropic Python SDK version 0.28.0 or later:
. Earlier versions do not expose the

Identify the static portion of your prompt: the system prompt, tool definitions, or document blocks that do not change between requests. Only content that is genuinely identical across requests should be marked for caching.
on the last content block of the prefix you want to cache. For a system prompt, this means passing a list of content blocks to the
parameter rather than a plain string, with the last block carrying the cache control marker.
Verify the prefix meets the minimum token count: 1,024 tokens for Claude 3.5 Sonnet and Claude 3 Opus, or 2,048 for Claude 3 Haiku. The API silently ignores
if the threshold is not met — check
in the response to confirm the cache was written.
Structure requests so the cached prefix comes first, followed by the dynamic content (user messages, tool results). The order matters: anything after the last
in API responses. A healthy caching setup should show mostly
after the first request in a window.
For multi-turn conversations, you can mark a checkpoint in the message history as cacheable by placing
on the last user message content block. This caches the entire conversation up to that point.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Build a long system prompt that is identical across all requests.
# Pass it as a list of content blocks so we can attach cache_control.
SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a senior software architect assistant with deep expertise in
distributed systems, cloud infrastructure, and API design. Your role is to help
engineering teams make sound architectural decisions. You provide structured analysis,
weigh trade-offs, and ground recommendations in operational reality rather than hype.

When evaluating options:
- Identify the specific problem and its constraints before proposing solutions.
- Consider failure modes, scalability ceilings, and operational burden.
- Be explicit about your assumptions and where data would change your recommendation.
- Prefer proven patterns over novel ones unless there is a clear, demonstrable advantage.
"""

def ask(question: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=[
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": SYSTEM_PROMPT,
                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},  # cache this block
            }
        ],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}],
    )
    usage = response.usage
    print(
        f"cache_creation={usage.cache_creation_input_tokens} "
        f"cache_read={usage.cache_read_input_tokens} "
        f"input={usage.input_tokens} "
        f"output={usage.output_tokens}"
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# First call — cache miss, prefix is written
answer1 = ask("Should we use Kafka or RabbitMQ for our event pipeline?")

# Second call — cache hit, prefix tokens charged at ~10%
answer2 = ask("How do we handle schema evolution in an event-driven system?")
Caching tool definitions alongside the system prompt

Scenario: a legal-tech platform lets users upload contracts and ask multiple natural-language questions about them — "What is the termination clause?", "Does this include an arbitration requirement?", "Summarize the IP ownership section". Each document is 8,000–15,000 tokens. Without caching, every question re-sends the full document, resulting in tens of thousands of input tokens per session. With caching, the document is cached on the first question and subsequent questions pay only for the query itself.

import anthropic
from pathlib import Path

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def analyze_contract(contract_text: str, questions: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    """
    Ask multiple questions about a contract, caching the document
    so only the first request pays full document input cost.
    """
    answers = []

    for i, question in enumerate(questions):
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=1024,
            system=[
                {
                    "type": "text",
                    "text": (
                        "You are a legal analysis assistant. Read the contract below "
                        "and answer questions accurately based only on its content. "
                        "Quote relevant clauses when supporting your answer.\n\n"
                        "CONTRACT:\n" + contract_text
                    ),
                    # The contract is identical for every question — cache it.
                    "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},
                }
            ],
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}],
        )

        usage = response.usage
        hit = usage.cache_read_input_tokens
        miss = usage.cache_creation_input_tokens
        status = "MISS (write)" if miss > 0 else "HIT"
        print(f"  Q{i+1} [{status}] cache_read={hit} cache_write={miss} input={usage.input_tokens}")

        answers.append(response.content[0].text)

    return answers


# Example usage
contract = Path("contract.txt").read_text()  # a 10,000-token contract

questions = [
    "What is the effective date and term of this agreement?",
    "Does this contract include a non-compete clause? If so, what are its terms?",
    "What are the payment terms and late payment penalties?",
    "Under what conditions can either party terminate the agreement early?",
    "Who owns intellectual property created during the engagement?",
]

print("Analyzing contract...")
answers = analyze_contract(contract, questions)

# Expected output:
#   Q1 [MISS (write)] cache_read=0  cache_write=10000  input=10050
#   Q2 [HIT]          cache_read=10000  cache_write=0  input=10035
#   Q3 [HIT]          cache_read=10000  cache_write=0  input=10028
#   Q4 [HIT]          cache_read=10000  cache_write=0  input=10040
#   Q5 [HIT]          cache_read=10000  cache_write=0  input=10032
#
# Total input tokens saved on questions 2-5: ~40,000 tokens → ~4,000 token-equivalents
# Cost reduction on the document portion: ~90% for the last 4 questions.

This pattern generalizes to any workflow where a large, stable document or context accompanies many smaller dynamic inputs: code review over a large codebase, RAG over a fixed reference corpus, structured data extraction from a database dump, or compliance checking against a regulatory document.
Placing dynamic content inside the cached block.

If you include the user's name, a timestamp, a session ID, or any per-request variable inside the cached prefix, the prefix hash changes on every request and the cache never hits. Verify that everything before the
marker is byte-identical across all requests.

Many teams enable caching and assume it is working without confirming via the usage fields. A missed minimum-token threshold or a prefix that is unexpectedly dynamic will cause silent cache misses. Always log
Caching a prefix below the token minimum.
on a 500-token system prompt when using Claude 3.5 Sonnet (1,024-token minimum) silently has no effect. The response shows zero for both cache fields, and you pay full price on every call.
Relying on caching for sparse traffic.

If your API traffic averages one request every ten minutes, the cache expires before the next request arrives. You pay the write surcharge each time without accruing hits. Consider batching requests within a single session or running a lightweight "cache warmer" request to keep the TTL alive.
Expecting caching to survive model version changes.
to a new model version invalidates the cache, because the KV tensors are model-specific. Budget for re-warm costs when upgrading model versions.
Placing the cache_control marker in the middle of a block list incorrectly.
The API caches content up to and including the block marked with
markers are allowed, creating multiple cache checkpoints, but incorrectly ordering them can result in caching less context than intended.

Structure your prompts so stable content (system instructions, tool definitions, documents) precedes all dynamic content (user queries, per-session context). The cacheable block must be a genuine prefix of the request.
Consolidate tools into the system prompt if they are large.
parameter can also be cached by placing
on the last tool definition. For very large tool sets, this reduces costs significantly on tool-heavy agents.
Use separate cache checkpoints for layered prefixes.

markers at different points in your prompt. For example, cache the system prompt at one checkpoint and a large retrieved document at a second checkpoint. Each checkpoint has its own TTL and cache key.
Monitor the cache hit rate in production.
across a rolling window. A hit rate below 80 % for a steady-traffic system suggests the prefix is not as stable as expected, or traffic has dropped below the TTL threshold.
Keep the cached prefix truly stable.

Treat the cached prefix like compiled code: review changes carefully, as every change expires the existing cache and triggers a new write across all in-flight requests. Version-control your system prompts.
Warm the cache before expected traffic bursts.

If you anticipate a surge (product launch, scheduled batch job), fire a single dummy request with the correct prefix just before the burst begins. This pre-warms the cache and ensures all burst requests are hits.
Account for write cost in your pricing model.

The 25 % write surcharge is paid once per TTL window, not once ever. For a system that restarts traffic every six minutes, the cache never pays for itself. Run the break-even calculation: write cost / (per-hit saving) = minimum requests needed per window.
The cost model for prompt caching works as follows. Let
be the number of prefix tokens,
be the number of requests in a five-minute window, and
Without caching: total cost = N × P × c (all tokens on every request).

With caching: total cost = P × 1.25c (write, once per window) + (N - 1) × P × 0.1c (hits). Break-even occurs at N = 1.39 — fewer than two requests per window. Any sustained traffic above this breaks even.

At 1,000 requests per five-minute window against a 4,000-token prefix, caching reduces prefix cost from 4,000,000 token-equivalents to approximately 404,000 (write: 5,000 + 999 hits: 399,600). That is a 90 % reduction on the prefix portion.

For a 4,000-token prefix, the reduction in prefill computation is substantial. The model skips ≈ 4,000 attention forward passes. At 200 tokens/second prefill throughput, this saves roughly 20 seconds of prefill latency per request on a busy GPU.

The cache is stored server-side by Anthropic. Adding more API clients or scaling out your own application does not affect cache behavior — all clients sharing the same API key share the same cache. This is genuinely stateless scaling from your application's perspective.

For agents that progressively build context (plan phase, then execute phase), you can checkpoint the plan and then checkpoint the accumulated execution context separately, getting incremental savings as the conversation grows.
Full support via cache_control on content blocks and tools
Full support via cache_control on content blocks
Passthrough via ChatAnthropic; cache_control in extra_headers or bind_tools
Supported via underlying Anthropic SDK; configure in LLM node parameters
Compatible; pass cache_control through the underlying client call
Shows cache_creation and cache_read in usage panel
Reports cached vs. non-cached token usage in per-request logs
Instrument cache_creation_input_tokens and cache_read_input_tokens as span attributes for cache hit rate dashboards

Prompt caching and RAG address different problems. RAG reduces the size of context that needs to be sent by retrieving only relevant chunks; caching assumes the full context is sent and reduces the cost of sending it repeatedly. They are complementary: you can RAG to select a relevant document and then cache the selected document across multiple questions about it.

Prompt caching does not introduce new attack surfaces, but it does concentrate sensitive content in persistent server-side storage. If your system prompt or cached document contains proprietary information, credentials, or PII, that content lives in Anthropic's infrastructure for the duration of the cache TTL (up to five minutes after the last hit). This is operationally similar to any API request, but worth noting for compliance reviews.

Cache entries are strictly isolated per API key. There is no mechanism by which one organization's cached content can be accessed by another organization's requests. Two API keys with identical prompts maintain independent caches.

The ephemeral cache type guarantees that cached KV tensors are evicted no later than five minutes after the last cache hit. There is no long-lived persistent cache that accumulates data across days or weeks.

A cached response is numerically identical to a non-cached response. There is no way for a downstream consumer or attacker to infer, from the model's output alone, whether a given request was served from cache. This prevents timing-based inference attacks.

A cache miss is not an error — it is simply a more expensive, slower request. Your application must be designed to handle the full token cost and latency on every request, treating cache hits as an optimization, not a guarantee. Do not set API timeouts assuming cached latency.

If you cache user-supplied content (a document uploaded by the user), and that document contains injected instructions, those instructions persist in the cache. Apply prompt injection defenses — clear delimiters, instruction-following constraints in the system prompt — before caching any user-supplied text.

Prompt caching stores the transformer KV attention states for a static prefix, so subsequent requests skip prefill computation on those tokens and pay only ~10 % of the normal input-token cost for the cached portion.
Mark the cacheable boundary by placing
on the last content block of the stable prefix. Everything before this marker is cached; everything after is processed normally.
The break-even point is fewer than two requests per five-minute TTL window — any sustained traffic above that threshold generates net savings on every cache hit.

The prefix must be byte-identical across requests. Even a single character of dynamic content in the prefix causes a cache miss and a new write surcharge. Audit every element of the cached block for per-request variability.
Cache hits improve TTFT as well as cost — the model loads precomputed KV tensors instead of recomputing prefill over thousands of tokens, reducing the delay to the first output token.
in every response to confirm caching is active. Silent misses (wrong threshold, dynamic prefix) are common and invisible without logging.
Prompt caching and RAG are complementary: RAG reduces what context is sent; caching reduces how much repeated sending costs. Use both in document-heavy agentic systems.
Design your application to handle cache misses gracefully — set timeouts and cost budgets for the full uncached request, and treat cache hits as a performance bonus rather than an assumption.
